title: Artificial Intelligence
tagline: From a 1950 thought experiment to machines that act on our behalf — the whole story, layer by layer.

events.title: The Turing Test
events.tagline: Can a machine make you believe it is human?
events.content:
Alan Turing publishes **"Computing Machinery and Intelligence"**, opening with a question he immediately replaces with a sharper one:

> "I propose to consider the question, 'Can machines think?' … replaced by the imitation game."

Instead of arguing about definitions of *thinking*, Turing proposes a test: if a machine can hold a conversation indistinguishable from a human's, the philosophical question stops mattering.

## Why it matters
- It reframes intelligence as **behavior**, not mechanism — a move the whole field inherits.
- Nearly every chatbot debate since, including the reaction to [[ChatGPT]], replays this argument.
- It set a benchmark culture: AI progress would be measured by tests and games for decades.

events.title: "AI" is coined
events.tagline: A summer workshop gives the field its name.
events.content:
At the **Dartmouth Summer Research Project**, John McCarthy — with Marvin Minsky, Claude Shannon and Nathaniel Rochester — coins the term **artificial intelligence** in the funding proposal:

> "Every aspect of learning or any other feature of intelligence can in principle be so precisely described that a machine can be made to simulate it."

Ten researchers spend the summer at Dartmouth College sketching how machines might use language, form abstractions, and improve themselves.

## Why it matters
- The name itself was a **bet**: that intelligence is describable, hence computable. Compare [[The Turing Test]] — behavior over mechanism.
- It created a *field* with an identity, funding story, and research agenda.
- Most of the founders believed human-level AI was a generation away. The over-promise → disappointment → "AI winter" cycle starts here.

events.title: Deep Blue
events.tagline: A machine defeats the world chess champion.
events.content:
IBM's **Deep Blue** beats Garry Kasparov 3½–2½ — the first defeat of a reigning world champion by a machine under tournament conditions.

Deep Blue evaluated roughly **200 million positions per second**. It didn't *understand* chess; it searched it. That distinction — brute search versus learned intuition — frames the next era: when [[AlexNet]] arrives, the balance tips from hand-built search toward learning.

## Why it matters
- First mainstream proof that a machine could beat the best human at a "thinking" task.
- Cemented the pattern of **games as milestones** (checkers → chess → Go → StarCraft).

events.title: AlexNet
events.tagline: Deep learning wins, and everything changes.
events.content:
A three-person team — Alex Krizhevsky, Ilya Sutskever, Geoffrey Hinton — enters a deep **convolutional neural network** into the ImageNet competition and wins by an unheard-of margin (15.3% error vs 26.2% for second place).

The recipe: a decades-old idea (neural nets), two new ingredients (**GPUs** + huge labeled datasets scraped from [[the-web/www-proposal|the Web]]), and the conviction to scale it.

## Why it matters
- Overnight, "neural networks" went from unfashionable to *the* method.
- The scaling lesson — more data, more compute, better results — leads directly to [[Transformers]] and ultimately [[ChatGPT]].
- Two of the three authors go on to found or lead the labs of the LLM era.

events.title: Transformers
events.content:
Eight Google researchers publish **"Attention Is All You Need"**, introducing the *Transformer* — an architecture that drops recurrence entirely and relies on **self-attention**: every token can directly look at every other token.

## Why it matters
- It parallelizes beautifully on GPUs → training can scale to internet-sized data.
- It is the **"T" in GPT**. Every modern large language model, including [[ChatGPT]], is a descendant.
- Its children conquered not just text but images ([[Vision Transformer]]), audio, code and biology.

*Open this event's timeline to follow the lineage in detail.*

children.title: Attention Is All You Need
children.content: The paper that started it: attention layers + feed-forward blocks, no recurrence, trained on translation. Its famous title was a half-joke that became the most-cited sentence in modern AI.

children.title: GPT-1
children.content: OpenAI's **GPT-1** takes the opposite bet from BERT: predict the *next* token, generatively, and scale it. At 117M parameters it is modest — but the line GPT-1 → GPT-2 → GPT-3 → [[ChatGPT]] is a straight application of the same recipe at growing scale.

sources.title: Improving Language Understanding by Generative Pre-Training (PDF)

children.title: BERT
children.content: Google's **BERT** pretrains a Transformer on masked-word prediction over Wikipedia, then fine-tunes it for any task. It tops 11 benchmarks at once and powers Google Search within a year — the first mass deployment of Transformer language understanding.

children.title: Vision Transformer
children.content: **ViT** splits an image into patches and feeds them to a standard Transformer — no convolutions. Given enough data it beats the best CNNs, proving the architecture is general-purpose. This is the lineage behind GPT-4's image understanding ([[GPT-4V]]).

events.title: ChatGPT
events.tagline: AI goes mainstream: 100M users in two months.
events.content:
On **November 30, 2022**, OpenAI releases a "research preview" of a chatbot built on GPT-3.5. It reaches **1 million users in 5 days** and ~100 million in two months — the fastest-growing consumer product in history at the time.

## What actually shipped
Under the hood it was not a new model but a new *interface*: a [[Transformers]]-based LLM fine-tuned with **RLHF** (reinforcement learning from human feedback) to follow instructions, wrapped in a plain chat box anyone could use.

## Why it matters
- It moved AI from research demos into **daily life** within weeks.
- It triggered the industry pivot: Google declared "code red", Microsoft invested $10B in OpenAI, and every major lab reoriented around LLMs.
- It restarted the [[The Turing Test]] debate in public, at dinner-table scale.
- Its rapid version history — see [[GPT-4]] — became the cadence the whole industry marches to.

*Open this event's timeline for the version-by-version story.*

children.title: GPT-3.5
children.content: The model behind the original launch — GPT-3 refined with instruction-following and RLHF. Free, fast, and good *enough* to astonish: the gap between "research curiosity" and "useful assistant" closed here.

children.title: GPT-4
children.content:
**GPT-4** lands four months after launch and resets expectations: top-decile scores on the bar exam, strong code generation, far fewer hallucinations, and (later) the ability to see images.

It also begins the *productization* era — system prompts, function calling, longer context — the raw material that [[GPT-4 Turbo]] and [[DevDay 2023]] turn into a platform.

*This event has its own timeline — open it to follow GPT-4's evolution.*

children.title: GPT-4 launch
children.content: Launched March 14, 2023 alongside a technical report famous for what it *didn't* say — no parameter count, no architecture details. The era of competitive secrecy begins.

children.title: GPT-4V
children.content: GPT-4 with vision rolls out: screenshots, charts, photos, handwriting. The conceptual ancestor is the [[Vision Transformer]] — patches in, tokens out, one architecture for everything.

children.title: GPT-4 Turbo
children.content:
Announced at OpenAI's first developer conference: a faster GPT-4 at a third of the price with a **128K-token context window** — entire books in a single prompt.

The platform story matters as much as the model — see [[DevDay 2023]] for what shipped around it.

children.title: DevDay 2023
children.content:
OpenAI's first DevDay packs a year of platform strategy into one keynote. The theme: GPT-4 stops being a chatbot and becomes **infrastructure**.

*Open this event's timeline for the announcements, one by one.*

sources.title: New models and developer products announced at DevDay — OpenAI

children.title: 128K context window
children.content: Context jumps from 8K to **128K tokens** — about 300 pages. Whole codebases and contracts fit in one prompt, unlocking retrieval-light workflows.

children.title: Custom GPTs
children.content: Anyone can package instructions + files + tools into a shareable **GPT** — no code. The first mass-market experiment in personalized agents, and a precursor of [[The age of agents]].

children.title: Price cuts
children.content: GPT-4 Turbo input tokens priced ~3× lower than GPT-4. The cost curve of intelligence keeps falling — the quiet enabler of every AI product business model.

children.title: Turbo 2024-04-09
children.content: A refreshed Turbo with vision built in and majorly improved instruction-following — the workhorse model until GPT-4o replaces it a month later.

children.title: GPT-4o
children.content: "o" for *omni*: one network handles text, vision and **realtime voice** with ~300ms latency — conversation at human speed. The assistant stops feeling like a terminal and starts feeling like a presence.

children.title: Reasoning models
children.tagline: Models that think before they answer.
children.content: The o-series and successors introduce **test-time reasoning**: the model deliberates in a hidden chain of thought before answering. Math, code and science benchmarks jump again — and the technique spreads industry-wide within months, feeding directly into [[The age of agents]].

events.title: The age of agents
events.content:
The frontier shifts from *chat* to *action*: models that plan multi-step work, call tools, browse, write and run code, and operate computers — checked on by humans rather than driven by them.

## Why it matters
- The unit of value moves from "a good answer" to **"a finished task."**
- Interfaces invert: instead of you visiting [[ChatGPT]], agents visit your tools.
- Built on two prior layers: [[Transformers]] for capability, [[Reasoning models]] for reliability.

*Open the timeline to see the building blocks land.*

children.title: Tool use matures
children.content: Structured tool calling becomes a first-class capability across every major model — the hands that reasoning needed.

children.title: Computer use
children.content: Models learn to drive real GUIs — clicking, typing, reading screens — turning any software into an API.

children.title: Multi-agent teams
children.content: Orchestrators spawn specialist sub-agents that research, write and review in parallel. Workflows start to look like *org charts*.
